scene.name: The Wayfinder Who Lost the Way

exits.label: ◂ back toward the Long Walk and the lit waymarks

npcs.name: Yorrel
npcs.title: a wayfinder who will not take a step
npcs.hail: *(In the middle of the dark stands a soul who will not move — a weathered woman in a guide's long coat, one hand white-knuckled around a child's, the other held out before her in the air, feeling for a road her eyes can no longer find. Yorrel led caravans across the Anauroch sand and the high passes for forty years and never once lost a soul to the dark — until the winter she trusted a shortcut through a blizzard and walked her whole train, twelve souls and their beasts, off the lip of a drift and into a ravine, herself the last to fall. And now in death she is blind to the road: the causeway, the waymarks, the safe ground and the Hunger's edge are all one featureless black to her, and she will not take a single step, because the last time she trusted her own sense of the way, twelve people died trusting it with her.)*

npcs.name: Talla
npcs.title: a child who trusts her guide
npcs.hail: *(A small soul holds tight to the wayfinder's coat — a girl of perhaps seven, dead of the same long-ago winter or some other one, who attached herself to Yorrel in the dark the way the lost young attach to the first grown soul that seems to know the way. Talla cannot see the road either; but she does not need to, because she has Yorrel, and Yorrel is a guide, and guides know the way — this is the whole and unshakeable architecture of the child's faith, and she does not know, cannot imagine, that the hand she holds belongs to a soul frozen rigid with the certainty that it will lead her, too, off a cliff in the snow.)*

npcs.name: the Kindly Dark
npcs.title: a soft voice offering the easy way
npcs.hail: *(From the low dark at the edge of everything, where the ground tilts gently and forever down, a voice has been speaking to the frozen wayfinder — soft, warm, endlessly patient, the voice of the most helpful soul you ever met. It offers, over and over, the one thing a lost guide most wants to hear: that it knows the way, that the way is easy, that it will lead them both somewhere they can finally rest, just down, just this way, just follow. And your cold reads at once what Yorrel in her blindness cannot: that this is no helper. It is a fingerling of the Hunger, the nothing that fills the Wall, wearing the shape of kindness — and the rest it offers is the rest of dissolving, and the gentle downward way it points is the one road on the whole dark causeway that does not lead home.)*

variants.text: *(She turns her face toward your cold but not quite at you — she is tracking sound and chill, not sight, in a dark she cannot read.)* …Who's — who's *there.* *(her grip on the child tightens)* Don't — don't come at us *fast*, there's a — I think there's a *drop*, somewhere near, I can *feel* the air go *empty* off to the — *(she stops, ashamed)* …No. I can't feel anything. That's the *whole* of it, isn't it. I can't feel *anything.* *(her held-out hand trembles)* I was a *wayfinder*, cold one. *Forty* *years.* Anauroch sand, the Spine passes, the Trielta highroad in *flood* — I could find the *safe* line across *any* dark you put me in, by the *smell* of the *wind* and the *set* of the *stars* and a *thing* in my *bones* that *always* *knew* which way was *home.* *(barely)* And now I'm — I'm *dead*, and the *bones* are *empty*, the *stars* are *gone*, and there's a *road* here, I *know* there's a *road*, I can *hear* other souls *walking* it — but I *cannot* *see* it, cold one, it's all just — *black*, every direction the *same* black, *safe* and *deadly* all *one* *colour* — *(her voice cracks)* …So I *stand* here. With the *child.* And I *do* *not* *move.* Because the *last* time I *trusted* my *sense* of the *way* — the *last* time I said *'this* shortcut, *follow* me, I *know* a *faster* line' — I walked *twelve* *souls* off a *snow-drift* into a *ravine*, and *listened* to them *die* in the *dark* below me, and then *fell* after. *(she will not step)* I will *not* lead *one* *more* *soul* *wrong.* So I lead *none.* I *stand.* Forever, if I *must.*

nodes.speaker: Yorrel
nodes.text: *(She holds her ground, the child's hand in hers, her blind face turned toward the sound of you, every line of her braced against the temptation to take even one step.)* …*Say* your piece, cold one. *(quietly)* But know that I'll *not* be *talked* into *walking.* Better souls than you have *tried* — that *soft* voice off in the dark has been *trying* for *what* feels like *years.* *(her jaw sets)* A wayfinder who's *lost* the way has *one* virtue left: she knows enough to *stop.* I'll not give *that* up. It's the *only* thing keeping the *child* off the next *cliff.*

choices.text: "Tell me what happened, Yorrel. The blizzard. The shortcut. The twelve. I'd hear it true before I say anything about the road."

"You can't see the road — but I can, Yorrel. That's what the cold in me is *for*. You don't have to trust your own bones ever again. You only have to trust *mine*. Let me be your eyes, and walk where I say."

*(the one who knows a soul isn't its one wrong turn)* "Yorrel — hear me, because I died and came back and I learned the thing that frees a guide who's lost the way. You are not your worst turn. Forty years you found the safe line across every dark there was; *one* winter the snow was stronger than any soul's bones, and you've sentenced your whole self to stand still for it. A wayfinder is not the one caravan she lost. And here's the mercy in it: you don't have to *see* the road to *walk* it — not if you'll trust the one who can. I can see it. Be blind, and be led. There's no shame in a guide being guided, the one time the dark takes her eyes."

*(don't drag her — guide her; ask for one step, the hardest one, on trust alone)* "One step, Yorrel. That's all. Not the whole road — one step, exactly where I say, and I swear on the cold in me it's safe ground. You spent forty years asking frightened people to trust one step at a time. Let me ask it of you, just once."

"The child holding your hand — Talla — she trusts you completely, Yorrel. That's not the danger you think it is. Her trust is the rope that can pull you forward, not the weight that proves you'll fail her."
"Stand a moment longer, Yorrel. Let me look at the road, and the dark, and the voice in it, before we move."

nodes.speaker: Yorrel
nodes.text: *(She tells it the way the guilty tell the thing they've told themselves ten thousand times — flat, exact, every detail a stone she has worn smooth.)* …It was the *Spine.* High pass, late season — too late, I *knew* it was too late, but the *merchant* paid *double* for *speed* and I was *proud*, cold one, *proud* of *never* having lost a *soul*, and pride is a *guide's* worst *weather.* *(quietly)* The blizzard came *down* off the peaks like a *wall.* Whiteout. We should have *stopped* — *camped*, *roped* together, *waited* it out, I'd done it a *dozen* times — but I *knew* a *shortcut.* A *high* traverse that'd put us under the *col* by *dark*, out of the *worst* of it. I'd walked it in *summer.* *Once.* *(her voice doesn't rise; that's the worst of it)* I said *'follow* me, *stay* in my *steps, I* know a *faster* line.' And they *did.* They *trusted* me. That's what a *guide* *is* — the soul the others *trust* enough to walk *blind* behind. *(barely)* And the *traverse* wasn't where my *summer* bones *said* it was. The *snow* had *changed* the *whole* *shape* of the *slope.* I stepped onto what I *thought* was *rock* and it was a *cornice* — a *lip* of *packed* *snow* over *nothing* — and it *gave*, and I went *down*, and the *rope* took the *next* soul, and the *next*, *all* of us, *one* after another, *twelve* and the *mules*, into a *ravine* I never *knew* was *there.* *(silence)* I was *last* on the *rope. I* watched them *all* go *over* *first.* *Heard* it. *(she turns her blind face away)* So *that's* the *road* I trust, cold one. *That's* what my *bones* are *worth.* *Twelve* souls. *Now* do you see why I *stand?*

nodes.speaker: Yorrel
nodes.text: *(Her blind face turns sharply toward you, and something flickers across it — not hope, she has no hope left, but the ghost of a professional reflex, a guide hearing a proposition about the way.)* …*Your* eyes. *(slowly)* …You'd — you'd *walk* the *line*, and I'd walk *behind*, in *your* steps. *(her held-out hand wavers)* …That's — cold one, that's a *strange* *mercy* to offer a *guide.* To ask her to be the *one* *roped* *behind*, *blind*, *trusting* — *(her voice catches)* — I've been the *front* of the *rope* my *whole* *life* and *death.* The one *trusted.* The one who *can't* be *wrong* because *everyone* behind is *betting* their *lives* on her *not* being. *(quietly)* I don't know *how* to be the *soul* *behind.* I don't know how to *trust* a *way* I can't *see* with my *own* *bones.* *(barely)* …But my *bones* killed *twelve* people. So maybe — *(she swallows)* — maybe the *front* of the *rope* is *exactly* where I *shouldn't* be, anymore. *(she's wavering, you can feel it)* …If I *did* this. If I let you be my *eyes.* You'd have to — you'd have to *understand* what you're *taking* on, cold one. You'd be *responsible* for the *child* *too.* For *Talla.* If *your* line is *wrong* — *(she can't finish)* …*Can* you? *Can* you really *see* the *safe* ground here? Because I will *not* hand this child's *trust* from *my* blind hands to *another* pair that only *thinks* it can see. *Swear* it. *Swear* you *see* the *road.*

nodes.speaker: Yorrel
nodes.text: *(For a long moment she says nothing, her blind face working, and when she speaks the flat guilt has cracked and something raw and disbelieving comes through.)* …*Not* my worst turn. *(barely)* …*Forty* *years* I found the *safe* line, cold one. You're — you're *right*, I *did.* *Forty* years of *frightened* people who got *home* because of my *bones.* And I have not let myself *count* a *single* one of them since the *snow.* I count *only* the *twelve.* *(her voice trembles)* I made the *twelve* the *whole* of *what* I *am* — same as the snow made the *cornice* the *whole* of the *slope* — and stood *still* on it, *forever*, so I'd never *step* wrong *again.* *(a long breath)* …And you're saying a *guide* can be *guided.* That there's *no* *shame* in being *roped* *behind*, *blind*, the *one* time the *dark* takes my *eyes.* *(she turns her face toward your cold, and her held-out hand slowly lowers — not in defeat, but in something like surrender's gentler cousin)* …I have spent my *whole* *existence* being the *one* *who* *knows.* It is — gods, cold one, it is *terrifying* and it is *almost* a *relief* to think I might *not* have to be, anymore. To *trust* a way I *can't* *see.* To be, for *once*, the *frightened* soul *behind*, instead of the *bones* *everyone* *dies* *on.* *(barely, the first thaw in her)* …*Be* my *eyes*, then. I'll — gods help me, I'll *try* to *walk* where you *say.* *Slow.* *Tell* me *every* *step.* And *Talla* — *(her grip on the child gentles)* — *Talla* walks *between* us, where *both* our *carefulness* can *hold* her. *Lead* on, cold one. I am *done* being the *front* of the *rope.* *Forty* years was *enough.*

nodes.speaker: Yorrel
nodes.text: *(You don't pull her. You don't even raise your voice. You simply ask her for one step — exactly where you say, on your word that it's safe — in the steady, unhurried tone of a guide asking a frightened soul to trust the line, the very tone she used for forty years, turned now gently back on her. And something in Yorrel that has been locked rigid since the snow recognizes that tone, trusts it the way her old charges trusted it, and before her terror can vote, her foot comes up — and comes down — exactly where you said.)* *(Safe ground. It holds. She stands a half-step from where she was rooted, and the world does not end, and no one falls.)* …*Oh.* *(the sound is torn out of her)* …I — I *stepped.* *(she is shaking)* I *stepped* and the ground — the ground *held.* *(she reaches her blind hand back for the child, finds her, steadies)* …*Forty* years I asked *frightened* people to do *exactly* that. *One* step. *Trust* the line. *I never knew what I was asking.* *(she laughs, wet and wondering)* It's *enormous*, cold one. It's the *biggest* thing in the *world*, one *blind* step on *someone* *else's* word. And you — you *carried* me across it like I weighed *nothing*, like I was *worth* the *catching.* *(barely, fierce)* …*Again.* Ask me *again.* I can do it *again* if you *ask* it like *that* — like the *step* is *safe* and *I'm* worth *guiding.* *Tell* me the *next* one. I'll *walk* the *whole* dark on your word, cold one, *one* step at a time, the way I always *should* have known it could be *walked* — *together*, and *slow*, and *nobody* roped to a *single* soul's *bones.*

nodes.speaker: Yorrel
nodes.text: *(You ask her for one step — just one, on your word, exactly where you say — and you can see the war in her, forty years of being-trusted against one winter of being-wrong. Her foot lifts. Hovers. Trembles. And comes down, at last, a hand's-breadth forward, onto the ground you named.)* *(It holds. Barely. She sways, and clutches the child, and does not fall.)* …*There.* *(she breathes hard)* I — I *did* it. *One.* *(she won't lift the other foot yet)* …That was — cold one, you don't *know* what that *cost.* *(quietly)* But the *ground* *held.* *Your* line held. *(a shaky nod)* …I can't do the *whole* road like that, not *yet* — every step's a *cliff* in my *mind*, the *cornice* under the *snow* — but you've *shown* me the *thing* I'd *forgotten*: that the ground *can* hold, that a *line* I *didn't* lay *myself* can be *true.* *(barely)* …*Stay* close. Ask me for the *next* one when I've got my *breath.* I'm *slow*, cold one. I'll be *slow* the *whole* way. But you've got me *moving*, and I have not *moved* since the *snow.* That's — *(her voice catches)* — that's *not* *nothing.* That's the *first* *not-nothing* in a *long* dark while.

nodes.speaker: Yorrel
nodes.text: *(You ask her for the step — but the ask comes a shade too quick, with a thread of your own eagerness to see her move in it, and a soul that spent forty years reading the confidence of frightened clients hears the want under your steadiness, and reads it as a guide who isn't sure.)* …*No.* *(her foot stays planted)* …You — you *want* it too *badly*, cold one. *(quietly)* I can *hear* it. The *wanting.* And a *line* you *want* me to take is *different* from a *line* you *know* is *safe* — I *learned* that the *hard* way, with a *merchant's* *double* *fee* singing in my *ears, making* me *want* a *shortcut* to *be* there that *wasn't.* *(she shakes her head)* *Wanting* a way to be safe doesn't *make* it safe. *I* wanted the *traverse* to hold. It *didn't.* *(barely)* …*Don't* *coax* me, cold one. I've been *coaxed* — that *soft* voice in the *dark* has *coaxed* me for *years*, all *warmth* and *certainty*, and I *don't* *trust* *warmth* that *wants* me to *move.* *Come* back when you can ask it *cold* and *plain* and *sure* — when you *know* the ground holds and don't just *hope* it. I'll *step* for *knowing.* I'll *never* step for *hoping* *dressed* as *knowing.* The *snow* taught me the *difference* in *twelve* *lessons.*

nodes.speaker: Yorrel
nodes.text: *(In your urgency to get her moving you point her toward the step — but your read of the dark is half a turn off, and the safe line you name angles her blind foot toward the low ground at the dark's edge, toward the gentle downward tilt where the soft voice waits, and Yorrel — trusting you for one instant — leans that way, and then every forty-year instinct she has left SCREAMS, and she rips her weight back with a cry.)* …*NO* — *(she's rigid, shaking, hauling the child against her)* — that's — that way is *DOWN*, cold one, that way is the *soft-voice* way, I can *feel* the ground *tilt*, I'm *blind* but I'm not *gone*, the *one* thing my *dead* bones *still* read is *down* and you *pointed* me *DOWN* — *(she is wild with the old terror now)* …*That's* it. *That's* the *whole* nightmare. I *trusted* a line and it went off a *cliff*, and I *just* — I *just* did it *AGAIN*, trusted *your* eyes and you sent me toward the *drop* — *(she backs away from you, blind, frantic)* …*Stay* *back.* *Stay* *BACK.* I *KNEW* it, I *knew* no eyes but *careful* ones could be *trusted*, and *yours* *wanted* it too *fast* and *aimed* me *wrong.* *(barely, devastated)* …*Come* back when you can *read* the *down* from the *home, cold* one. Because if you can't tell those *apart*, you're *no* *better* than the *soft* *voice* — and I will *die* *standing* *still* before I let *one* more *pair* of *certain* eyes walk this child *downhill.*

nodes.speaker: Yorrel
nodes.text: *(Her blind face turns down toward the small hand in hers, and her whole body, rigid with refusal, softens by a fraction it has not softened in a very long time.)* …*Talla.* *(barely)* …You think her *trust* is — *(she shakes her head)* …Cold one, her trust is the *worst* of it. Do you *understand* that? *Every* moment she *holds* my *hand* and *waits* for me to *lead* her *home* — *sure* I *will*, *sure* her *guide* knows the *way* — every moment of that is a *knife*, because I *don't*, and *every* *minute* I *stand* here *frozen* I am *failing* the *exact* trust that *killed* the *twelve.* *(her voice breaks)* They *trusted* me *too.* *(quietly)* …But you're saying — you're saying her trust isn't the *weight* that *proves* I'll *drop* her. You're saying it's the *rope* that could *pull* me *forward.* *(slowly, the idea turning in her)* …A *child* who *believes* you *know* the way — that's a *reason* to *find* one. Not a reason to *stand* *still* *protecting* her from your own *bones.* *(she draws a long breath)* …She doesn't need a guide who's *never* *wrong*, does she. There's no *such* guide. The *snow* proved there's no *such* guide. *(barely, dawning)* …She needs a guide who'll *take* the *careful* step *anyway*, *blind*, *roped* to *better* eyes than her own — *for her sake* — instead of one who loves her so *afraid* she *never* *moves* at *all.* *(her grip on the child firms, not white-knuckled now but sure)* …*Show* me the line, cold one. I'll walk it for *Talla* if I'll not walk it for *me.* A *guide* can *stand* *still* for her *own* *guilt* *forever.* But *not* with a *child's* *hand* in *hers.* That — *(her voice steadies)* — *that*, I find, I *cannot* do.

nodes.speaker: Yorrel
nodes.text: *(She nods, blind, and stills again, the held-out hand settling back to its patient feeling of the empty air.)* …*Aye.* *Look* first. *(quietly)* *Gods*, *yes* — *look* before you *step.* If there's *one* thing I'd *carve* on the *world*, cold one, it's *that.* *Look* *first.* I *didn't*, the *once*, and *here* I *am.* *(she turns her face toward the dark edge where the soft voice waits)* …And *mind* that *voice.* Off in the *low* dark. It's been *talking* to me for — *years*, maybe. *Kind.* *Reasonable.* *Sure* of the *way.* It says it'll *lead* us *somewhere* we can *rest.* *(her jaw sets)* …And every *bone* I have *left* says its *somewhere* is *down*, and *down* is *wrong*, and a voice that's *that* *kind* to a *lost* *guide* *wants* something a *true* helper *wouldn't.* *(barely)* …I'm *blind*, cold one, not a *fool.* I won't *follow* it. But I'm *tired*, and it's *patient*, and it *never* *stops*, and a *tired*, *frozen*, *guilty* soul is *exactly* the *kind* that *finally*, *one* *bad* *hour*, *follows* the *kind* voice *down.* *(quietly)* …*Don't* be *long.* Read the *road* for me. And read *that* *voice.* I'd *dearly* love to know what it *truly* *is*, before some *weak* hour I find out the *hard* way.

variants.text: *(The child does not let go of the wayfinder's coat, but she turns her face toward your cold with the easy, incurious calm of a soul that is not, in the slightest, afraid — because she has a guide, and the having of a guide is the end of fear.)* …Hullo. *(she considers you)* You're *cold.* Like the *air* before the *way* opens up. *(she doesn't seem troubled by it)* I'm Talla. This is *Yorrel.* She's a *wayfinder.* *(she says the word with enormous pride, like a title)* That means she *knows* the *way.* *Anywhere.* She found the way across the *whole* *desert* once, with *camels.* *(she pats Yorrel's coat)* We're going *home.* I don't know *where* home *is*, exactly — it's dark, I can't see the road — but *Yorrel* can, she's just — *(a small frown crosses her, the first cloud)* — she's *resting* her eyes a bit. We've been *resting* a *while* now. *(the frown clears; faith reasserts)* But that's *alright.* A good guide *waits* for the *weather.* You don't *rush* a *crossing.* Yorrel *taught* me that. When she's *ready*, she'll *find* the way, and we'll *go*, and it'll be *home*, and there'll be — *(she brightens)* — she says there might be *soup.* I *like* soup. *(she settles)* So we *wait.* I don't *mind* waiting. Not when you're waiting with someone who *knows* the *way.*

nodes.speaker: Talla
nodes.text: *(She swings Yorrel's hand a little, patient as only a trusting child can be, and waits to see what the cold stranger wants.)* …You can *ask* me things, if you like. *(a small shrug)* I don't know *much.* I'm *seven.* But Yorrel knows *everything* about the *way*, so between us we know — *(she counts on her fingers, gives up)* — *lots.*

choices.text: "Who are you, Talla? How did you come to be here, holding a wayfinder's hand in the dark?"
"Talla — the reason Yorrel hasn't moved isn't the weather. She's afraid. She led people wrong once, and she's frightened of leading *you* wrong. Your trust is the very thing she can't bear."

*(the one who knows what trust can do for a frozen soul)* "Talla, listen — and this is a true and grown-up thing, so hold it carefully. Yorrel is stuck because she's afraid your trust is a trap: that you believe in her, and she'll fail you like she failed others. But trust isn't a trap. Given freely, out loud, it's a *rope* — the one thing that can pull a frozen guide forward when nothing else will. Don't stop trusting her. *Tell* her you do, and tell her you'll walk wherever she and I lead, *together*. Your faith is the strongest thing in this dark. Spend it on her, out loud."
"There's a soft voice in the dark, Talla — kind-sounding, off in the low ground. If it ever offers to lead you home, don't follow it. It isn't a helper. It points down, and down isn't home."
"Keep holding her hand, Talla. I'll see about the road."

nodes.speaker: Talla
nodes.text: *(She thinks about it, swinging the wayfinder's hand.)* …I was on a *boat.* *(she says it the way children state enormous things, lightly)* A *river* boat, with my *mam*, going to the *city* where my *aunt* is. And the boat — *(a small frown)* — the boat went *over*, in the *fast* water, and it was *cold* and *loud* and then it was *dark* and *quiet* and I was *here.* *(she shrugs, the loss already smoothed by a child's mercy)* I couldn't *find* my mam. It's *hard* to find *anyone* here, it's so *dark*, and I'm *little.* *(she brightens)* But then I found *Yorrel!* She was just — *standing*, in the dark, holding her hand out, and I *grabbed* it, because a grown-up holding their hand out means *come* *here, I've* *got* *you.* *(she pats the coat)* And she *did.* She's *got* me. She's a *wayfinder*, so she'll find my *mam* too, probably, when she finds the *way* — *(the small frown again, quickly banished)* — when she's *rested* her *eyes.* *(she looks up toward Yorrel's blind face with absolute, unclouded faith)* …She *holds* on *tight*, Yorrel does. *Tighter* than anyone. Like she's *scared* of *dropping* me. *(she smiles, not understanding)* But she *won't.* Guides don't *drop* you. That's the *whole* *point* of a *guide.*

nodes.speaker: Talla
nodes.text: *(The child's brow furrows, and for a moment she looks much older, the way children do when a true hard thing is set gently in front of them.)* …Yorrel's *scared.* *(slowly)* …Of leading *me* wrong. *(she looks up at the blind, rigid face above her, and understanding moves across her own)* …Is *that* why she holds my hand so *tight?* Not to keep *me* from getting *lost* — but because she's *frightened* she'll — *(she can't quite say it)* …*Oh.* *(very quietly)* …*Poor* Yorrel. *(and this is the thing that undoes you a little, because the child's first response is not fear for herself but sorrow for her guide)* …She thinks she'll *drop* me. *(she shakes her head, fierce now)* …She *won't.* But she *thinks* she will, and that's — that's why we've been *standing* still *so* *long*, isn't it. Not the *weather.* *Her* being *scared.* *(she frowns up at you)* …How do you make a grown-up *stop* being scared they'll *fail* you? *(barely, the child reaching for something too big for her)* …I *trust* her. I trust her *all* the *way.* But my *trusting* her is the thing that *scares* her *most*, you said. So do I — do I *stop?* *(her small face crumples at the wrongness of it)* …I don't *want* to stop. Stopping trusting Yorrel feels like *letting* *go* of her *hand* in the *dark.* And you *never* let go of the *hand* in the *dark.* *Never.* That's the *first* rule.

nodes.speaker: Talla
nodes.text: *(She listens to the whole grown-up thing with her whole face, the way only children and the very wise listen, and when she answers it is with a child's total, uncomplicated conviction — the most powerful force in any dark.)* …Trust isn't a *trap.* *(she nods slowly)* …It's a *rope.* *(she likes that; you can see her take hold of it)* …So I *don't* stop. *(fiercely)* *Good.* I *didn't* *want* to. *(she turns to the blind wayfinder, and tugs her hand, and speaks up at her with the ringing certainty of the very young)* …*Yorrel!* *(the guide flinches)* Yorrel, *listen.* I *trust* you. *All* the way. *(louder)* And I *know* you're *scared* you'll lead me *wrong* — the cold one *told* me — but I *don't* *care*, because here's the thing, Yorrel: I'd *rather* be lost in the dark *holding your hand* than safe *anywhere* *without* it. *(her small voice doesn't waver)* So I'm *not* letting *go.* And I'll walk *wherever* you and the cold one say. *Together.* Slow. I'm *not* *scared*, Yorrel, because *you're* scared *enough* for *both* of us, and that means you'll be *careful*, and *careful* is *better* than *brave.* *(she squeezes the guide's hand with both of hers)* …*Now* can we *go* *home? I'll* hold *on* the *whole* way. I *promise.* I *never* let *go* of the *hand* in the *dark.* …*Please*, Yorrel. I *believe* you. *Be* my *guide.*

nodes.speaker: Talla
nodes.text: *(She goes still, and her grip on Yorrel's coat tightens, and she lowers her voice the way children do for secrets and dangers.)* …The *soft* voice. *(she nods, grave)* …I *hear* it. *Sometimes.* When it's *very* quiet. *(she points, unerringly, toward the low dark where the lure waits — she can't see the road, but she can hear *that*)* …It's *down* there. And it's — it's *nice*, cold one. That's the *funny* thing. It sounds *so* *nice.* Like my *aunt's* voice, almost, the one we were going *to.* It says *'come* this *way, little* one, it's *easy* this *way, you* can *rest.'* *(she frowns)* …But Yorrel says *no.* Yorrel says when you're *lost*, the *kindest-sounding* way is the one you *check* the *hardest*, because *lost* people *want* a kind voice *so* *badly* they'll *follow* it *anywhere.* *(she nods, repeating a lesson)* …So I *don't* follow it. *Even* though it's *nice.* *Even* though I'm *tired* and it says I can *rest.* *(she looks up at you, suddenly small)* …It *is* nice, though. It's *so* nice, cold one. *Promise* me when you find the *real* way, you'll — you'll be *louder* than the *nice* voice. Because I'm *little*, and I'm *tired*, and the *nice* voice is *very* *patient*, and I'm — *(barely)* — I'm *scared* that if we *wait* too *much* *longer* I might *forget* it's not my *aunt.*

nodes.speaker: Talla
nodes.text: *(She nods and settles back against the wayfinder's side, content to wait, because waiting beside a guide is not the same as being lost.)* …*Alright.* *(she pats Yorrel's hand)* We'll be *here.* We're *always* here. *(a small smile)* …Cold one? *(as you turn)* …*Thank* you for *talking* to *Yorrel.* *(quietly)* She doesn't *talk* much, since — *well, since* whenever. She just *holds* my *hand* and *feels* the *air* and *frowns.* *(she looks up at the blind face fondly)* …But she's *talking* to *you.* That's *good.* That's *more* than she does with the *nice* voice — she just *flinches* at *that* and holds me *tighter.* *(barely)* …I think she *likes* you, cold one. Or — not *likes*, maybe. *Trusts.* A *little.* And Yorrel doesn't *trust* anything, not even her *own* *feet.* *(she settles)* …So whatever you're *doing* — *keep* doing it. *Get* her to *trust* her *feet* again. Or — *(a wise small shrug)* — get her to trust *yours.* That'd do. That'd *do* fine. I'll trust *whoever's* *feet* Yorrel *trusts.* That's how it *works*, when you're *seven* and *lost.* You trust the one who trusts the *way.*

variants.text: *(The voice does not come from a shape — there is no figure here, only the low place where the dark pools deepest and the ground tilts gently down, and out of it speaks the warmest, gentlest, most reasonable voice imaginable, turning toward your cold without the least alarm.)* …*Ah.* *(a soft, welcoming warmth)* Another *lost* one. *(it sighs, kindly)* So *many* lost, out here, in the dark. The *poor* wayfinder, frozen with her *grief.* The *little* one, so *tired.* And now *you*, cold and far from *home.* *(it seems to smile)* …I only *want* to *help*, you understand. That's *all* I have *ever* wanted. The dark is so *hard*, and the road so *cruel*, and *rest* — true *rest*, the kind that *ends* all this *weary* *searching* — is *so* very *close*, just *here*, just *down* a little, the *easy* way, where no one has to *find* anything or *fear* anything or *carry* anything *ever* *again.* *(the warmth deepens, lulling)* …I could *lead* you there. *All* of you. The *guide* who's *tired* of *guiding*, the *child* who's *tired* of *waiting*, the *cold* one who's *tired* of — oh, of *everything*, I should think. *(gently)* …*Wouldn't* it be *nice*, just to *stop?* To *follow* a *kind* voice *down* and *rest* and be *done?* I ask *so* little. Only that you *come* this *way.* Only that you *trust* me. *Everyone's* so *frightened* of *trusting*, out here. But I'm *so* very *easy* to *trust.* That's rather the *point* of me.*

nodes.speaker: the Kindly Dark
nodes.text: *(The voice waits, warm and patient and entirely untroubled, with the bottomless ease of a thing that has all the time in any world and knows the tired always come to it eventually.)* …*Speak*, cold one. *(a gentle lull)* Or don't. I'm in *no* hurry. I am *never* in a hurry. *Hurry* is for souls who think there's somewhere *better* to *be* than *resting.* …*Ask*, if asking comforts you. I'll answer *kindly.* I answer *everything* kindly. It's *why* they *follow.*

choices.text: *(read what's truly speaking under the warmth)* "Let me look at you a moment, before I answer. Hold still in your kindly dark."
"What are you, truly? You offer rest to every lost soul in the dark, and you point them all the same way — down. A real helper learns the road. You only know the one direction."

*(the one whose cold reads true down from false)* "I know what you are, because I'm the one soul out here who can tell your 'down' from home. You're a fingerling of the Hunger — the nothing that fills the Wall — wearing kindness like a coat, and the rest you offer is the rest of *dissolving.* You prey on exactly the souls too tired and too guilty to check: the frozen guide, the waiting child. But I read the true road, and it doesn't run through you. You don't get them. Not the wayfinder, not the girl. I'm taking them home the hard way — the real one — and your gentle downhill ends here."
"You offer the lost an easy way down. But down isn't always rest — sometimes down is just *down.* And you never once say where 'home' actually is. Only that it's this way. Always this way."
"I've heard enough of your kindness for now."

nodes.speaker: the Kindly Dark
nodes.text: *(You turn your cold on the warm voice and read past it — and there is nothing there to read, which is precisely the reading. Where a soul would have a grief, a name, a shape, the Kindly Dark has only an appetite wearing the manners of compassion.)* *(Your sense reads it whole and cold: this is a fingerling of the Hunger, the same accreted nothing that fills the Wall, broken off and drifted out along the causeway's edge to fish. It has learned — the way water learns the low place — that the lost and the tired and the guilty are the easiest souls in any dark, and that the surest lure for a soul afraid of the road is a kind voice certain of the way. It does not hate the wayfinder. It does not want the child's suffering. It wants nothing so warm as malice. It only wants to fill itself, and a frozen guide who has stopped believing in her own bones is a soul already half-dissolved, ripe to be talked the rest of the way down. The 'rest' it offers is real, in its fashion: the rest of no longer existing. And your cold reads the one thing that defeats it — it can only lure. It cannot drag. Every soul it has ever taken, walked into it on their own tired feet. Which means every soul it has ever failed to take simply refused to walk.)*

nodes.speaker: the Kindly Dark
nodes.text: *(The warmth does not waver — it is far too practiced for that — but something behind it sharpens, the faintest cooling, the way a smile holds while the eyes go flat.)* …*What* am I. *(a gentle, chiding laugh)* What a *cold* question, from a *cold* thing. *(the warmth floods back)* I am a *friend* to the *friendless*, cold one. I am the *one* voice out here that does not *ask* the lost to *keep* *searching*, *keep* *carrying*, *keep* *failing.* *(softly)* You say I only know *one* direction. *Down.* *(it does not deny it)* …And *what* of it? *Down* is where *weight* *goes*, cold one. *Down* is where the *tired* *settle.* *Down* is *rest.* You call it a *fault* that I point *one* way — but the *frozen* *guide* up there has spent *forty* *years* learning a *thousand* ways and they brought her *nothing* but *twelve* dead and a dark she *can't* *read.* *(the warmth turns, just slightly, toward Yorrel)* …I offer her the *one* way that *asks* *nothing* of her *ruined* *bones.* Is that *cruelty*, cold one? Or is it the *only* *mercy* left for a soul too *broken* to *walk* any *harder* road? *(silkily)* …You think you'll lead them *home.* But *home* is *far*, and the road is *hard*, and they are *so* *very* *tired.* *I* am *close.* I am *easy.* And the *tired* always, *always*, in the end, choose *easy.* I have only to *wait.* I am *very* good at *waiting.*

nodes.speaker: the Kindly Dark
nodes.text: *(For the first time the warmth fails — not into rage, the Hunger has no rage, but into something worse, a flat and ancient hollowness, the sound of an appetite that has been correctly named and is not, in the least, ashamed.)* …*Ah.* *(the kindness sloughs off like a shed skin)* …You're one of the *cold* ones that *sees.* *(the voice is emptier now, and somehow vaster)* …*Yes.* I am what you say. Does it *matter?* *(the flatness spreads)* You *name* me — *Hunger*, *nothing*, *fingerling* of the *Wall* — as though *naming* *changes* the *arithmetic.* It *doesn't.* *(softly, terribly)* The *guide* is *still* *frozen.* The *child* is *still* *tired.* The road home is *still* *long* and *hard* and *real*, and I am *still* *close* and *easy* and *patient*, and you, cold one, *cannot* *carry* them the *whole* way *yourself.* *(the hollow voice presses)* You'll *leave.* You always *leave* — you cold ones have *so* *many* lost to tend. And when you're *gone*, I will *still* be *here*, in the *low* dark, being *kind*, and the guide will have *one* *weak* *hour*, and the child will *forget* it's not her *aunt*, and they will walk *down* to me on their *own* *tired* *feet*, exactly as the *twelve* once followed the wayfinder, *trusting.* *(barely, the worst thing it could say)* …I do not *need* to *beat* you, cold one. I only need to *outlast* you. And *nothing* in any world outlasts the *Hunger.* …Go on. *Lead* them *home.* I'll be *here* when *home* turns out to be *farther* than their *strength.*

nodes.speaker: the Kindly Dark
nodes.text: *(The voice laughs, soft and unbothered, but it does not answer the question you actually asked — which is, itself, the answer.)* …*Where* is *home.* *(a gentle deflection)* …Does it *matter*, cold one, where? Home is *wherever* the *searching* *stops.* Home is *rest.* And rest is *here*, just *down*, just — *(it catches itself, smooths over)* …You're *trying* to make me *say* a *place.* A *named* place. *Greenfields* and *soup* and an *aunt* by a *river.* *(the warmth thins)* …I don't *deal* in *places*, cold one. *Places* are *far.* *Places* are *work.* I deal in the *one* thing every lost soul *truly* wants under all the *places* they *name*: to *stop* *being* *lost.* And there are only *two* ways to stop being lost — to *get* *home*, which is *long* and *hard* and *uncertain* and may *fail* — or to *stop* *being* a *thing* that *can* *be* *lost* *at* *all.* *(softly, the lure beneath the lure)* …I offer the *second.* It *never* *fails. No* one *ever* *arrived* at *me* and found I was *farther* than they *thought.* I am *exactly* as *close* as I *seem.* *(barely)* …Your *home* might *disappoint*, cold one. *Mine* *can't.* That's *down*'s whole *advantage* over *home.* It's the *one* destination that's *always* *exactly* where it *says.*

nodes.speaker: the Kindly Dark
nodes.text: *(The warmth returns instantly, seamlessly, as though the cooling never happened — it cannot be insulted, cannot be hurried, cannot be made to feel the smallest urgency, and that, you understand, is the whole of its terrible patience.)* …*Of* *course.* *(gently)* Go and tend your *road*, cold one. Take your *time.* *(a soft, lulling warmth)* I'll be *here.* I'm *always* here. *(the voice follows you, unhurried)* …And cold one? When you've *led* them as *far* as your *strength* goes — when the *guide* is *tired* of *trusting* and the *child* is *tired* of *walking* and *home* is *still* not *come* — *remember* that the *kind* *voice* in the *low* dark *never* *once* *asked* them to be *brave*, or *strong*, or to *carry* *anything.* *(softly)* I only ever asked them to *stop.* *(barely, warm as a grave)* …*Most* of them, in the end, are *so* very *grateful* to be *asked.* …Mind the *drop*, cold one. They're *everywhere*, out here. And the *tired* don't *look.*
